Philippines' GDP Growth Accelerates to 5.9% in Third Quarter of 2023
Despite significant market and geopolitical challenges, the Philippines has demonstrated
remarkable resilience and progress, according to a report by the National Economic and
Development Authority (NEDA). The country’s economy expanded by 5.9% in the third quarter of
2023, solidifying its position as one of the fastest-growing major emerging economies in Asia
and among the leading destinations for IT-BPM services. This represents a recovery from the
second quarter’s GDP growth of 4.3%.
In a press conference, the NEDA revealed that the GDP growth in Q3 2023 was driven by all major
economic sectors. The services sector, inclusive of the IT-BPM industry, led the way with a strong
growth of 6.8%. This was followed by the industry sector at 5.5%, and the agriculture sector at
0.9%. Both domestic and international demand showed signs of recovery, with government
spending contributing 36% to the economic growth.
The NEDA further expressed optimism that the growth target of 6% to 7% for the entire year of
2023 will be met, provided the fourth quarter grows by at least 7.2%. The agency also assured
that the pursuit of liberalization reforms and public-private partnership projects will continue to
attract more investments and create more jobs.
When compared to other major IT-BPM service destination economies, the Philippines lags
behind India, which is projected to grow by 6.7% by the end of the year. Malaysia and Mexico both
reported a GDP growth of 3.3%. Poland’s economy also experienced a 0.4% economic expansion,
following a five-quarter recession that began in Q2 2022.
To support the GDP growth through the end of the year, the Philippines has developed strategies
to address high inflation and the potential impacts of El Niño, which could affect food production
and prices. NEDA also reaffirmed its commitment to the Philippine Development Plan 2023-2028,
which aims to achieve a stable, comfortable, and secure life for all Filipinos.

As the Philippines move into 2024, three critical factors have emerged in the third quarter that
could significantly influence economic outlook. These include the ongoing efforts to control
inflation rates, the necessity to maintain competitive interest rates, and the potential impact of
the impending El Niño phenomenon:
• High price level will impact economic growth. In October 2023, the country’s inflation rate
decelerated to 4.9%, a significant drop from the 6.1% recorded in September 2023. This
reduction in inflation was largely due to a lesser rise in the prices of food and non-alcoholic
beverages, which constitute the majority of the consumer basket, as reported by the
Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA). However, the inflation rate for October 2023 still
exceeds the government’s target, posing a potential risk to the country’s economic
expansion.
The International Monetary Fund (IMF) has recently adjusted its growth projection for the
Philippine economy in 2023, revising it downward to 5.3% from its earlier forecast of 6.2%.
This adjustment is primarily attributed to the high inflation negatively impacting consumer
demand, potentially necessitating the central bank to maintain elevated interest rates.
• Interest rates to remain high. The BSP kept its policy rate steady for the third consecutive
month at 6.25%. The decision to prolong the interest rates came in response to the upside
risks of inflation and the still weak national current account. Although rates are still high,
the BSP promises to act swiftly if necessary to raise rates to assist the stabilization of
inflation expectations. In order to determine its next course of action, the BSP will keep an
eye on the inflation outlook's upside risks, such as prospective wage increases, changes

IT-BPM Industry Forecasts a Promising Growth for 2023
A recent survey conducted by the Information Technology and Business Process Association of
the Philippines (IBPAP) has projected a bright future for the IT-BPM industry in the Philippines.
The survey anticipates an impressive growth rate of 8.8% for the industry in 2023, which is
expected to generate a revenue of US$35.4 billion. Remarkably, this growth rate outpaces the
global IT-BPM industry’s projected growth of 7.7%, which is forecasted to yield a revenue of
US$300 billion in 2023, according to a separate survey conducted by the Everest Group.
The IBPAP’s survey also anticipates that the IT-BPM industry in the Philippines will employ 1.7
million people in 2023, marking an 8.7% growth from the current headcount of 1.5 million. This
growth rate is higher than the global IT-BPM industry headcount growth forecast of 7.1%, which
is expected to reach 9.7 million in 2023.
These projections highlight the robust growth of the IT-BPM industry in the Philippines,
outperforming the global industry growth. The country continues to solidify its position as a
leading destination for IT-BPM services, contributing significantly to the nation’s economic growth
and employment.

Kearney Global Services Location Index 2023
The Global Services Location Index (GSLI) is published by the global management consulting firm
Kearney. The index ranks the top countries for outsourcing business services based on four
weighted dimensions:
1. Financial attractiveness (35%): cost of labor and infrastructure
2. People skills and availability (25%): quantity and quality of the talent pool
3. Business environment (25%): political, economic, regulatory, and cultural aspects that
affect the ease of doing business
4. Digital resonance (15%): digital skills of the labor force and digital outputs of business
activity
The 78 nations in the 2023 Global Services Location Index were chosen based on suggestions
from businesses, ongoing remote services activity, and government efforts to support the
industry. Below are the highlights of the IT-BPM services destination economies in the GSLI’s 2023
rankings:

Quick Facts: Some of the Philippine Economy’s Statistics
LABOR AND EMPLOYMENT
The Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) has reported an improvement in the nation’s employment
scenario. The unemployment rate for September 2023 stood at 4.5%, marking a decrease from
the 5% recorded during the same period in the previous year. This indicates a reduction in the
number of unemployed individuals in the country, with the figure declining from 2.5 million to 2.26
million.
Conversely, the employment rate saw a slightly upward trend, with an estimation of 95.5%, an
increase from the 95% recorded in the same month of the previous year. Furthermore, the
underemployment rate also showed a positive trend, decreasing to 10.7% in Q3 2023 from 15.4%
in the same period of 2022. These figures reflect the varying changes in employment numbers
across different industries.
These variations in the employment statistics could have an impact for the IT-BPM industry. A
decline in the unemployment rate could indicate a more competitive environment for talent
acquisition, which could potentially lead to an increase in wages and subsequently elevate
operational costs for IT-BPM companies.

APPROVED INVESTMENTS
In Q2 2023, the combined total of approved investments from foreign and Filipino nationals by
the Board of Investments (BOI) and the Philippine Economic Zone Authority (PEZA) at current
prices reached PHP 58 billion. This figure represents a 9% decrease compared to the combined
BOI and PEZA investments in Q2 2022. However, cumulative investments for the first half of 2023
have surged by 83% to PHP 465 billion from the same period in 2022.
The growth in approved investments is a direct result of the continued reopening of the economy,
particularly after President Marcos issued Proclamation No. 297 on July 21, lifting the State of
Public Health Emergency throughout the Philippines due to COVID-19. This, coupled with the
policy reforms enacted during the previous administration, such as the Corporate Recovery and
Tax Incentives for Enterprises (CREATE) Law, the Public Services Act, the Foreign Investments
Act, the Retail Trade Liberalization Act, and the allowance of 100 percent foreign investments in
renewable power, all contribute to the increasing attractiveness of the Philippines for foreign
investments. Additionally, the country's accession to the Regional Comprehensive Economic
Partnership (RCEP) agreement—considered the largest free trade deal of the century—is expected
to further boost approved foreign investments in the coming months.
According to the PSA, Japan was the largest contributor in terms of overall investment pledges,
contributing a total of PHP 20.36 billion. The next two countries were the Cayman Islands at
PHP 11.63 billion and Singapore at PHP 17.65 billion.

Empowering the Countryside:
Charting the Future of the Philippine IT-BPM Industry
The Philippine IT-BPM (Information Technology and Business Process Management) industry
stands at a pivotal crossroad, aiming to generate 1.1 million new jobs by 2028. Close to 600,000
of these opportunities are anticipated to emerge from the countryside, underscoring its crucial
role in the nation's economic landscape. This shift marks a significant departure from the
traditional urban-centric growth model, signaling a new era of distributed development and
inclusive growth.
Currently, the countryside accounts for 30% of the total workforce in the IT-BPM sector. With the
projected changes, this percentage is anticipated to rise to 40%.
The pandemic has acted as a catalyst in this transformation, accelerating shifts in work delivery
models and opening new possibilities for regions outside NCR. Remote work and digital
connectivity have broken down the geographical barriers, enabling talent from non-urban areas
to contribute significantly to the industry's growth.
Additionally, there's a strong governmental push to promote equitable economic growth across
the Philippines, moving beyond the NCR-centric development model. This approach is not only a
strategic move to leverage untapped potential but also a necessary step towards achieving
balanced national development. By channeling resources and attention to the countryside, the
Philippine government is laying the groundwork for a more inclusive economic future, where
prosperity is shared and sustainable across all regions.

Untapped Horizons: Harnessing Countryside Talent for IT-BPM
The countryside embodies a wealth of untapped potential, poised to significantly propel the
growth of the Philippine IT-BPM industry. With its expansive landscapes and large pool of
educated and skilled individuals, these locations present an ideal setting for businesses looking
to expand and flourish. Prior to the pandemic, a common trend was the migration of talent from
these regions to Metro Manila for employment opportunities. This trend is indicative of the latent
potential in the countryside, as a substantial portion of the workforce in the NCR is originally from
these provinces.
A compelling statistic highlighting this potential is that 75% of higher education graduates in the
Philippines hail from the countryside, mostly from region 4 CALABARZON, Central Visayas, and
Western Visayas. This demographic not only illustrates the vast talent pool available but also
strengthens the argument for directing IT-BPM industry growth towards these areas.
Moreover, the cost dynamics in the countryside further bolster its attractiveness for IT-BPM
industry expansion. The cost of living and operating a business in these provincial areas is often
considerably lower compared to major urban centers like Metro Manila. This economic advantage
enables companies to enjoy significant cost savings, which can be redirected towards expanding
operations and investing in talent development.
Another notable factor is the observed lower attrition rates in the countryside compared to the
NCR. This stability in the workforce is an asset for the IT-BPM sector, known for its dynamic and
often fluid employment landscape.
Additionally, encouraging the growth of the IT-BPM industry in the countryside addresses a critical
national concern: the high population density in Metro Manila. By providing viable employment
opportunities in non-urban areas, there is potential to alleviate the congestion in the capital,
leading to more balanced regional development and improved quality of life across the country.
This strategic shift not only promises economic benefits but also contributes to the broader
objective of achieving equitable and sustainable growth nationwide.
Navigating the Roadblocks: Overcoming Countryside IT-BPM Challenges
While the expansion of the IT-BPM industry into the Philippine countryside is laden with potential,
it also faces a multitude of challenges that need to be addressed to unlock its full potential.
One of the primary hurdles is capacity building. Empowering ICT councils and local government
units (LGUs) is crucial to creating an enabling environment for IT-BPM companies. These councils
are tasked with promoting their localities as viable investment hubs for ICT-enabled jobs and
industries. However, this requires a concerted effort in terms of resources, training, and strategic
planning to effectively attract and support IT-BPM investments.
Another key obstacle is the scarcity of comprehensive and location-specific data. This deficiency
in localized insights impedes the ability to make well-informed decisions and develop strategies
IBPAP Research Page 16

--- Page 18 ---
that are closely aligned with the unique characteristics of each area. To overcome this challenge,
it is imperative to empower local ICT councils, enabling them to take the lead in gathering
accurate and detailed data. Such information is vital for understanding the distinct needs,
resources, and potential of each locality, thereby facilitating more effective and efficient
strategies for both expansion and the establishment of new business spaces.
Political fluctuations pose an additional challenge. Changing leadership with each election cycle
can result in a loss of support for ongoing IT-BPM initiatives. In some locations, this can mean
starting from scratch with each new administration, leading to discontinuity and instability in
policies and support mechanisms. Such political uncertainties can be a deterrent for long-term
investments and planning.
Additionally, the expansion of the IT-BPM industry into the countryside is often constrained by
familiar infrastructural challenges, notably the shortage of suitable real estate and persistent
connectivity problems. These elements are essential to the foundation of any IT-BPM operation,
and their lack can considerably hinder the sector's growth in these areas.
To navigate these obstacles, a comprehensive and collaborative approach is needed, one that
involves persistent engagement from both public and private entities. This entails the formulation
of robust and adaptable strategies capable of enduring political shifts, committing to substantial
investments in infrastructural development, and cultivating an environment that prioritizes
ongoing data gathering and analysis. Such an approach is crucial not only for making informed
decisions but also for ensuring the successful expansion and sustainability of the IT-BPM industry
in the countryside.
Blueprint for Growth: Programs Empowering Countryside IT-BPM
The expansion of the IT-BPM industry into the Philippine countryside is supported by various
initiatives and programs, highlighting a collaborative effort between industry associations,
government bodies, and private enterprises.
Since 2009, IBPAP has fostered partnerships with the Department of Information and
Communications Technology (DICT), the National ICT Confederation of the Philippines (NICP),
and Leechiu Property Consultants to propel the Next Wave Cities program (now called the Digital
Cities program). This program aims to identify and develop areas outside Metro Manila as
potential IT-BPM hubs, focusing on infrastructure, talent development, and investment attraction.
The DICT has been instrumental in this endeavor, launching initiatives such as learning sessions,
roadshows, and enhanced institutional development workshops. Fairly recently, DICT Secretary
Ivan John Uy reaffirmed his commitment to nurturing the countryside, with a focus on job creation,
enhancing digital infrastructure, fostering digital skills development, and bolstering cybersecurity.
Large-scale infrastructure projects are also a cornerstone of the government's strategy to support
the IT-BPM industry's countryside expansion. President Ferdinand R. Marcos Jr.'s administration
has emphasized infrastructure as a key driver for achieving an ambitious economic growth target

Infrastructure Flagship Projects (IFPs) worth approximately PHP 8.7 trillion. These projects,
ranging from improving physical and digital connectivity to developing water, power, energy, and
transportation infrastructure, are critical to enhancing the attractiveness of the countryside for IT-

Lastly, major telecommunications companies like Globe, PLDT, and Converge are playing a vital
role in this expansion. Their initiatives to enhance connectivity and digital infrastructure are
fundamental to enabling the IT-BPM industry to thrive in the countryside.
Clark's Story: A Model of Countryside IT-BPM Success
Clark, once a significant U.S. military base, has undergone a remarkable transformation over the
years, becoming a bustling hub for the IT-BPM sector in the Philippines. Today, it stands as a
testament to successful urban and economic revitalization, with over 50,000 professionals
employed in the IT-BPM industry. This workforce forms the backbone of Clark's thriving economic
landscape, signifying a substantial shift from its historical roots to a modern, technologically
driven environment.

The Road Ahead: Solidifying the Countryside's Place in IT-BPM
How do we ensure the realization of creating 600,000 new jobs in the Philippine countryside by
2028? The answer lies in deepening collaboration among industry, government, and academia,
transcending conventional approaches to foster a more integrated environment. It demands a
concerted effort, a unified front where each stakeholder plays a pivotal role.
ICT councils and local governments must intensify their efforts, going beyond traditional
initiatives to actively encourage investors to choose their locations. This requires a proactive
stance in showcasing the unique strengths and opportunities that their cities offer, coupled with
a relentless drive to create a conducive environment for IT-BPM companies.
Moreover, it is crucial to leverage the current momentum of governmental support aiming to spur
economic growth in the countryside. This support should not just be seen as an advantage but
as a strategic tool to be actively utilized in attracting investments and fostering growth in the
countryside. The government's commitment to infrastructure development, particularly in Tier 2-
3 cities, opens a window of opportunity that must be capitalized upon.
The journey ahead is not just about the established Centers of Excellence like Cebu, Clark, and
Iloilo. The real test lies in the potential of Digital Cities to rise as formidable forces in the IT-BPM
arena. These emerging hubs hold the potential to not only contribute to the national employment
target but also to bring about a more balanced and inclusive economic growth across the country.
The future of the IT-BPM industry in the Philippine countryside is not just about reaching a
numerical target but about rewriting the narrative of regional development and economic
inclusivity. The question remains: are we prepared to embrace this challenge and usher in an era
of transformative growth that reshapes the economic landscape of the countryside? The answer
hinges on our collective actions and commitment in the coming years.

The Parties wish to explore a potential business relationship relating to mergers, acquisitions, strategic partnership, investment, or other transactions and related advisory work (the "Purpose"). In connection with the Purpose, each Party may disclose Confidential Information to the other Party.
1. Definition of Confidential Information
“Confidential Information” means any non-public information disclosed by or on behalf of a Party (the “Disclosing Party”) to the other Party (the “Receiving Party”), whether in oral, written, electronic, visual, or other form, that relates to the Disclosing Party or its affiliates, including business and operating information, financial information, pricing, forecasts, contracts, customer and supplier information, employee and compensation information, product or service information, technology, processes, trade secrets, know-how, strategies, plans, and the existence and status of discussions under the Purpose.
Confidential Information includes analyses, compilations, notes, and other materials prepared by the Receiving Party that contain or reflect Confidential Information.
2. Exclusions
Confidential Information does not include information that the Receiving Party can demonstrate by written records:
is or becomes publicly available through no breach of this Agreement;
was lawfully known to the Receiving Party without restriction prior to disclosure by the Disclosing Party;
is received from a third party who had the lawful right to disclose it without restriction; or
is independently developed by the Receiving Party without use of or reference to the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
3. Confidentiality Obligations
The Receiving Party shall: (a) keep the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information strictly confidential and protect it using at least the same degree of care it uses to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but no less than reasonable care; (b) use the Confidential Information solely for the Purpose; and (c) not disclose the Confidential Information to any third party except as permitted under Section 4.
4. Permitted Disclosures; Representatives
The Receiving Party may disclose Confidential Information to its directors, officers, employees, professional advisers (including legal counsel, accountants, tax advisers, and bankers), financing sources, and affiliates (collectively, “Representatives”) who have a need to know for the Purpose, provided that the Receiving Party ensures its Representatives are bound by confidentiality obligations no less protective than this Agreement. The Receiving Party remains responsible for any breach of this Agreement by its Representatives.
5. Compelled Disclosure
If the Receiving Party is required by law, regulation, court order, or governmental request to disclose any Confidential Information, it shall (to the extent legally permitted) provide prompt written notice to the Disclosing Party so the Disclosing Party may seek a protective order or other remedy. If such protection is not obtained, the Receiving Party shall disclose only the portion of Confidential Information that is legally required and shows reasonable efforts to obtain confidential treatment for any disclosed information.
6. No License; Ownership
All Confidential Information remains the property of the Disclosing Party. No license or other rights are granted to the Receiving Party by implication or otherwise under any patents, copyrights, trademarks, trade secrets, or other intellectual property rights by the disclosure of Confidential Information.
7. Return/Destruction
Upon written request by the Disclosing Party, or upon termination of discussions, the Receiving Party shall promptly (and in any event within five (5) business days) return or destroy all Confidential Information (including copies and extracts) in its possession or control, and certify such destruction upon request. Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Receiving Party may retain one archival copy solely to comply with legal, regulatory, or professional requirements, and any such retained copy remains subject to this Agreement.
8. No Obligation; No Warranty
Nothing in this Agreement obligates either Party to proceed with any transaction or business relationship. Any obligation to proceed shall arise only under a definitive written agreement executed by the Parties.
The Disclosing Party makes no representations or warranties, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Confidential Information. The Receiving Party agrees that it is responsible for its own evaluation and decisions, and that any reliance on Confidential Information is at its own risk.
9. Term and Survival
This Agreement commences on the Effective Date and remains in effect for two (2) years, unless terminated earlier by either Party upon written notice.
The confidentiality and use restrictions in this Agreement will survive for three (3) years from the date of each disclosure; provided that trade secrets will be protected for so long as they remain trade secrets under applicable law.
10. Remedies
The Receiving Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm for which monetary damages may be an insufficient remedy. The Disclosing Party is entitled to seek injunctive or equitable relief, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity.
11. Non-Circumvention (Limited)
During the term of this Agreement and for twelve (12) months thereafter, neither Party will knowingly use the other Party’s Confidential Information to directly circumvent the other Party by approaching, soliciting, or transacting with a third party that was introduced or identified through the other Party’s Confidential Information for the Purpose, without the introducing Party’s prior written consent. This Section does not restrict either Party from dealing with third parties independently known to it without use of the other Party’s Confidential Information.
12. Governing Law; Dispute Resolution
This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Republic of the Philippines, without regard to conflict of law principles.
The Parties shall attempt in good faith to resolve any dispute arising out of or relating to this Agreement through amicable negotiations for sixty (60) days before commencing formal proceedings. Nothing in this Section limits a Party’s right to seek urgent injunctive relief.

Entire Agreement. This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter and supersedes all prior discussions or understandings regarding confidentiality for the Purpose.
Amendments. Any amendment must be in writing and signed by authorized representatives of both Parties.
